---
title: "01 · Matrices and Matrix Operations"
subtitle: "Rectangular arrays of numbers — and how they multiply"
lesson-id: "t5.matrices"
tier: 5
prerequisites: ["t2.systems-linear-equations", "t4.vectors-2d-3d"]
unlocks: ["t5.linear-systems-gaussian", "t5.vector-spaces"]
applied-domains: ["ai-ml", "game-dev", "research"]
estimated-minutes: 30
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

1. Have you done [T2 L06 — Systems of Linear Equations](../../tier-2-prealgebra/06-systems-linear-equations/index.qmd)
   and [T4 L13 — Vectors](../../tier-4-precalc-calculus/13-vectors-2d-3d/index.qmd)?
2. Comfortable with adding and scaling vectors?
3. Have you noticed that systems of linear equations have a tabular
   structure of coefficients?
:::

## Touch

Pick two matrices. The widget computes their product.

```{ojs}
viewof matSel = Inputs.select(["I·v (identity × vector)", "[1 2; 3 4] × [5; 6]", "[1 0; 0 -1] × [3; 4] (reflect)", "[0 -1; 1 0] × [3; 4] (rotate 90°)"], {value: "I·v (identity × vector)", label: "Computation:"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  let str;
  switch(matSel) {
    case "I·v (identity × vector)": str = "[[1,0],[0,1]] × [v₁,v₂] = [v₁,v₂] (no change)"; break;
    case "[1 2; 3 4] × [5; 6]": str = "[[1,2],[3,4]] × [5,6] = [1·5+2·6, 3·5+4·6] = [17, 39]"; break;
    case "[1 0; 0 -1] × [3; 4] (reflect)": str = "[[1,0],[0,−1]] × [3,4] = [3, −4]  (flips y)"; break;
    case "[0 -1; 1 0] × [3; 4] (rotate 90°)": str = "[[0,−1],[1,0]] × [3,4] = [−4, 3]  (rotates 90° CCW)"; break;
  }
  return html`<div style="text-align:center; margin: 1rem 0;">
    <div style="font-family:monospace; font-size:1.2rem; color:#c2185b; font-weight:bold;">${str}</div>
  </div>`;
}
```

A **matrix** is a rectangular array of numbers. Matrix-vector
multiplication transforms a vector: rotating, scaling, reflecting.

## Wonder

1. The 2×2 identity matrix $I$ leaves any vector unchanged. Why is
   that the "identity"?
2. Multiplying $\begin{pmatrix} 0 & -1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$ by
   $(3, 4)^T$ gives $(-4, 3)^T$. That's a 90° rotation. Why?
3. A "transformation" of the plane is *the* role of a matrix. How?

## Discover

### Problem 1

A **matrix** is written $A = \begin{pmatrix} a_{11} & a_{12} \\
a_{21} & a_{22} \end{pmatrix}$ with rows and columns. **Sum:** add
componentwise. Compute $\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \end{pmatrix} +
\begin{pmatrix} 5 & 6 \\ 7 & 8 \end{pmatrix}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\begin{pmatrix} 6 & 8 \\ 10 & 12 \end{pmatrix}$.

Just like vectors, matrices add componentwise. Both must be the same
size.
</details>

### Problem 2

**Matrix-vector multiplication.** $A v$ takes a column vector $v$ and
produces a new column vector. Each entry of $Av$ is the **dot product**
of a row of $A$ with $v$.

Compute $\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix}
5 \\ 6 \end{pmatrix}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Top entry: $(1, 2) \cdot (5, 6) = 5 + 12 = 17$.
Bottom entry: $(3, 4) \cdot (5, 6) = 15 + 24 = 39$.
$\begin{pmatrix} 17 \\ 39 \end{pmatrix}$.

A 2×2 matrix maps 2D vectors to 2D vectors. A 3×2 matrix maps 2D to
3D. In general, an $m \times n$ matrix maps $\mathbb{R}^n \to
\mathbb{R}^m$.
</details>

### Problem 3

**Matrix-matrix multiplication.** $AB$ has entries given by row of $A$
dotted with column of $B$.

Compute $\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix}
0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
The $(i, j)$ entry of $AB$ is row $i$ of $A$ dotted with column $j$
of $B$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$(1, 1)$ entry: $(1, 2) \cdot (0, 1) = 2$.
$(1, 2)$ entry: $(1, 2) \cdot (1, 0) = 1$.
$(2, 1)$: $(3, 4) \cdot (0, 1) = 4$. $(2, 2)$: $(3, 4) \cdot (1, 0) =
3$.

$AB = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 4 & 3 \end{pmatrix}$.

For $AB$ to be defined: number of columns of $A$ = number of rows of
$B$.
</details>

::: {.fun-fact}
The word **matrix** was coined by **James Joseph Sylvester** in 1850
(from the Latin for "womb" — a matrix gives birth to determinants by
striking out rows and columns). The systematic algebra — addition,
multiplication, the non-commutative product you just met — was laid
down by Sylvester's friend **Arthur Cayley** in his 1858 *Memoir on
the Theory of Matrices*. Cayley wrote that he had been led to the
idea "either directly from the notion of a determinant; or as a
convenient mode of expression of equations" — exactly the two paths
you have now taken.
:::

### Problem 4

Show that matrix multiplication is **not commutative**: compute
$\begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\
0 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$ and the other order.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\
0 & 0 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$.

$\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\
0 & 0 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$.

**Different results.** $AB \neq BA$ in general — this is the most
*surprising* feature of matrix algebra and the source of much of its
richness (e.g., in quantum mechanics).
</details>

### Problem 5

The **identity matrix** $I_n$ is the $n \times n$ matrix with 1s on
the diagonal, 0s elsewhere. Show $I_2 v = v$ for any 2-vector.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$I_2 \begin{pmatrix} v_1 \\ v_2 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \cdot
v_1 + 0 \cdot v_2 \\ 0 \cdot v_1 + 1 \cdot v_2 \end{pmatrix} =
\begin{pmatrix} v_1 \\ v_2 \end{pmatrix}$.

So $I v = v$ for any vector. **$I$ is the identity for matrix
multiplication**: $A I = A = I A$ for any (compatible-size) $A$.
</details>

::: {.key-insight}
A matrix isn't really a *table* of numbers — it's a *transformation*
in disguise. Multiplying by a matrix takes every vector and moves
it; the columns of the matrix tell you exactly where the basis
arrows land. Once you know where the basis goes, the whole space's
motion is determined.
:::

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

A **matrix** is a rectangular array $A = (a_{ij})$ with $m$ rows and
$n$ columns ($m \times n$).

**Matrix sum:** $(A + B)_{ij} = a_{ij} + b_{ij}$ (same-size matrices).

**Scalar multiplication:** $(c A)_{ij} = c a_{ij}$.

**Matrix-vector product:** for $A$ ($m \times n$) and $v$ ($n$-vector),
$(Av)_i = \sum_j a_{ij} v_j$. Result: $m$-vector.

**Matrix-matrix product:** for $A$ ($m \times n$) and $B$ ($n \times
p$), $(AB)_{ij} = \sum_k a_{ik} b_{kj}$. Result: $m \times p$.

**Properties:**

- Associative: $(AB)C = A(BC)$.
- Distributive: $A(B + C) = AB + AC$.
- **Not commutative:** $AB \neq BA$ in general.

**Identity matrix** $I_n$: 1s on diagonal, 0s elsewhere. $AI = IA =
A$.

**Transpose** $A^T$: swap rows and columns. $(AB)^T = B^T A^T$ (the
order flips).

:::

> *Matrix = rectangular array; multiplication is "row dot column";*
> *Composition of transformations = matrix product;*
> *Order matters — $AB \neq BA$ in general.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> Compute $A B$ where $A = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ -1 & 3 \end{pmatrix}$
> and $B = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 0 \\ 1 & 5 \end{pmatrix}$.

$(AB)_{11} = 1 \cdot 4 + 2 \cdot 1 = 6$.
$(AB)_{12} = 1 \cdot 0 + 2 \cdot 5 = 10$.
$(AB)_{21} = -1 \cdot 4 + 3 \cdot 1 = -1$.
$(AB)_{22} = -1 \cdot 0 + 3 \cdot 5 = 15$.

$AB = \begin{pmatrix} 6 & 10 \\ -1 & 15 \end{pmatrix}$.

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** $\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix}
2 & 3 \\ 4 & 5 \end{pmatrix} = ?$

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\begin{pmatrix} 3 & 3 \\ 4 & 6 \end{pmatrix}$.
</details>

**W2.** $3 \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \end{pmatrix} = ?$

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\begin{pmatrix} 3 & 6 \\ 9 & 12 \end{pmatrix}$.
</details>

**W3.** $\begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 0 & 3 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\
2 \end{pmatrix} = ?$

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\begin{pmatrix} 4 \\ 6 \end{pmatrix}$.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** Compute $\begin{pmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ 2 & 3 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix}
2 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 7 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$.
</details>

**P2.** A rotation by 90° counter-clockwise has matrix $R = \begin{pmatrix}
0 & -1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$. Apply it to $(1, 0)^T$ and $(0, 1)^T$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$R (1, 0)^T = (0, 1)^T$. $R (0, 1)^T = (-1, 0)^T$.

**Standard basis vectors are sent to the columns of the rotation
matrix.** Crucial insight: a matrix's columns are *the images of the
basis vectors*. Once you know where the basis goes, you know the
whole transformation.
</details>

**P3.** Compute $R^2$ for $R = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & -1 \\ 1 & 0
\end{pmatrix}$. What does it represent geometrically?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$R^2 = \begin{pmatrix} -1 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{pmatrix} = -I$.

Two 90° rotations = 180° rotation = negation. The matrix $-I$ sends
every vector to its opposite. **Composing transformations =
multiplying matrices.**
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** **Why are 2D rotations and reflections matrices?** Show that
the rotation matrix by angle $\theta$ is $R(\theta) = \begin{pmatrix}
\cos \theta & -\sin \theta \\ \sin \theta & \cos \theta
\end{pmatrix}$. Verify that $R(\theta) R(\phi) = R(\theta + \phi)$
(angles compose by addition).

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$R(\theta) R(\phi) = \begin{pmatrix} \cos \theta \cos \phi - \sin \theta \sin \phi & -(\cos \theta \sin \phi + \sin \theta \cos \phi) \\ \sin \theta \cos \phi + \cos \theta \sin \phi & \cos \theta \cos \phi - \sin \theta \sin \phi \end{pmatrix}$.

Apply angle-addition identities (T3 L09):
$\cos(\theta + \phi), -\sin(\theta + \phi), \sin(\theta + \phi),
\cos(\theta + \phi)$ — exactly $R(\theta + \phi)$ ✓.

**Matrix multiplication of rotations = adding angles.** This is the
group structure of 2D rotations: $SO(2)$, isomorphic to the unit
circle in the complex plane.
</details>

**S2.** **Matrix multiplication is the workhorse of deep learning.** A
neural network layer computes $y = Wx + b$ where $W$ is a weight
matrix. For a layer with 1024 input neurons and 1024 output neurons,
how many multiplications does one forward pass cost?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$W$ is $1024 \times 1024$. $Wx$ has 1024 entries, each requires 1024
multiplications. **Total: $\sim 10^6$ multiplications per layer**.

A modern Transformer with hundreds of such layers, processing a
sequence of thousands of tokens, runs *trillions* of matrix-product
operations per forward pass. Training does another similar pass
backwards (chain rule).

This is why GPUs and TPUs exist: they are matrix-multiplication
engines, optimised for *exactly* the operation you just learned.
Efficient matrix multiplication algorithms (Strassen, Coppersmith-
Winograd) shave constant factors but the core idea is the same.
</details>

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"Matrix multiplication is just elementwise."** No — that's
   *Hadamard product* (rare in practice). The standard product is
   *row-by-column dot products*.
2. **"$AB$ requires $A$ and $B$ to be the same size."** No — they
   need *compatible* sizes: cols($A$) = rows($B$). Result has
   rows($A$) and cols($B$).
3. **"$AB = BA$."** Almost never. Be careful with order — many
   algebraic identities you know from numbers don't hold for
   matrices.
:::

### Takeaways

If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

1. **A [matrix](/glossary.qmd#matrix) is a transformation, not a
   table** — its columns are where the basis [vectors](/glossary.qmd#vector)
   land.
2. **Matrix multiplication is "row [dot product](/glossary.qmd#dot-product)
   column"** and corresponds to *composing* the underlying transformations.
3. **Order matters: $AB \neq BA$ in general** — the single fact that
   makes matrix algebra genuinely different from ordinary arithmetic.

### Self-Assessment

1. Could you multiply $\begin{pmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 3 \end{pmatrix}$
   by $(4, 5)^T$?
2. Without scrolling back, what does the matrix $\begin{pmatrix} 0 & -1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$
   do to a 2D vector?
3. Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 *Discover* problems and 4 of the 6
   practice problems without peeking past *Hint 1*?

(Answers: $(8, 15)^T$; rotates by 90° CCW.)

- **3 yes:** ready for **[02 · Linear Systems & Gaussian](../02-linear-systems-gaussian/index.qmd)**.
- **2 yes:** redo **S1** (rotation composition).
- **0–1 yes:** re-read *Discover P3 and P4*.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **[T2 L06 — Systems of Linear Equations](../../tier-2-prealgebra/06-systems-linear-equations/index.qmd)** —
  matrices encode systems compactly.
- **[T4 L13 — Vectors](../../tier-4-precalc-calculus/13-vectors-2d-3d/index.qmd)** —
  matrix-vector products generalise vector ops.

### What this unlocks

- **[02 · Linear Systems & Gaussian Elimination](../02-linear-systems-gaussian/index.qmd)**
- **[03 · Vector Spaces](../03-vector-spaces/index.qmd)**
- **[04 · Eigenvalues](../04-eigenvalues/index.qmd)**

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI / Machine Learning.** S2 — matrix multiplication is the central
operation. Modern AI training is *measured* in TFLOPs (trillions of
floating-point matrix operations per second).
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Game development & graphics.** Every 3D transformation (translate,
rotate, scale, project) is a matrix. Composing matrices = composing
transforms.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Mathematical research.** Matrices are the bridge from algebra to
geometry to analysis. Linear algebra is *the* most reused tier of
mathematics across the sciences.
:::
